test_text.title: Anita's Search for a Part-time Student Job

audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a conversation between a student and a job advisor.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Oh, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Is this... er... room number 26?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: So, is this the Student Job Center?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: It certainly is.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Well, actually, I'm looking for a job.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: A part-time job.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Do you have anything available at the moment?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: The student is looking for a part-time job, so part-time has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Oh, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Is this... er... room number 26?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: So, is this the Student Job Center?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: It certainly is.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Well, actually, I'm looking for a job.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: A part-time job.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Do you have anything available at the moment?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Are you a registered student?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: I'm afraid this service is only available to full-time students.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Yes, I am.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: I'm doing a degree in business studies.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Uh. Here's my student card.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Which year are you in?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Well, I've been at uni for 4 years, but I'm in the 3rd year because I took last year off.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Right, well, let's just have a look at what positions are available at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Uh. There's a job working at the reception desk at the Sports Center for three evenings a week.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: That's Wednesdays, Thursdays and Fridays.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Hmm. That sounds like fun, but unfortunately I have evening lectures, so that's not possible I'm afraid.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Is there anything during the day?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: OK, that's no good then.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Um. What about cleaning?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: There's a position for a cleaner.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: At the childcare center.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: But you'd need to be there at 6:00 am.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Does that appeal?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: 6 o'clock in the morning?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Oh, that's far too early for me.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: I'm afraid I'd never make it that early in the morning.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Hmm, well, there was a position going in the computer lab for 3 days a week that might be OK.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Ah, here it is.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: No, it's in the library, not the lab.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Uh. Clerical assistant required, I think it mostly involves putting the books back on the shelves.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Oh no, hang on.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: It's for Wednesday and Friday evenings again.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: No, I can't manage that because of the lectures.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: OK, I'm getting the idea.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Uh. Look, I'll just get a few details from you anyway, and then we can check through the list and see what comes up.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We'll fill in the personal details on this application form first, if that's OK.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Yeah, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Now, what's your name again?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Anita Newman.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Uh. That's NEWMAN.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And your address, Anita?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: I'm in one of the halls of residence for postgraduate students, you know International House.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: OK, that's easy.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Uh. What's your room number there?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Room B 5 6 9, uh, no, sorry, B 6 5 9.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: I always get that wrong.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: I haven't been living there very long.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Do you have any other skills?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Typing, languages, that sort of thing?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Well, I speak some Japanese.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Right, I'll make a note of that.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Now, let's see what else is available.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Uh. What do you think of administrative work?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: There's a position for an office assistant at the English Language Center.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Hmm, that sounds interesting.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: It's for three days a week, Monday, Friday, and Saturday mornings.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Interested?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Hmm. I was hoping to have Saturdays free, but I need the work, so, uh huh, can you tell me what the job involves?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Yep, sure.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: It says here that you'll be required to deal with student inquiries and answer the phone.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Oh, I'm sure I can handle all that without a problem.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Well, would you like me to arrange an interview for you?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Say Friday morning around 10.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Oh, uh, can we make it a bit later?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Unfortunately, I've got something to do at 10.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Would that be OK?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Not a problem.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: How about 11:30.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Hope it works out for you, Anita.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Me too, and thanks for all your help.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

answer_key.correct_answer: Sport Centre | Sports Centre | Sport Center | Sports Center

test_text.title: Sponsored Trekking for Sierra Education Charity

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a talk given by a group offering a walking holiday to raise money for charity.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, I'm very pleased to have been invited along to your club to talk about our charity sponsored walking holiday for education aid.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'll start by giving you a brief overview of what it entails.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. First of all, let me explain what we mean by sponsored here.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is where people promise to donate money to the charity if you achieve your goal, in this case to walk a certain number of miles.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Basically we are organizing a 10 day holiday, from the 6th to the 16th of November, with eight days actual walking, trekking in the Semira mountains.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's have a look at some of the details.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We require you to raise sponsorship money of at least $3,200 paying $250 of it upfront as a deposit.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the rest in stages throughout the year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Out of this about 35% will go on your expenses, and that leaves 65% guaranteed to go to the charity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which brings me to the most important part.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This trek is being specifically organized to help education in the Semira region.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Last year, we helped train teachers for the disabled.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this year we're focusing on the pupils.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Each of the walkers' sponsorship money will go to help an individual special needs pupil in one of the mountain schools.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the second part of the talk, I'll be giving you a lot more details.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But back to the basic information.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Age limits.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is the second time we've run this kind of holiday, and on the first we even had an 80 year old, but we found it was wise to establish limits this time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You have to be at least 18, and the top limit is now 70.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Though you need to obtain a health certificate from your doctor if you are over 60 years old.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, the Semira Mountains are among the highest in the world, but you mustn't be too daunted.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We will mainly be trekking in the foothills only, although there will be spectacular views even in the foothills.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, you will need to be extremely fit, if you aren't now, and you're interested in coming with us.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You have plenty of time to get into shape.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll be sleeping in tents, so you must have quite a bit of equipment with you, but you will be helped by local assistants.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Your bedding and so forth will be carried by them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We ask that you only walk with a small rucksack with needs for the day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I don't think I've really said enough about the marvelous area you'll be walking in.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's have a look at some of the sights you'll be seeing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Apart from these spectacular snow covered peaks and valleys, there are marvelous historic villages.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The area has been famous for centuries for making beautiful carpets, although recently there has been a trend to move into weaving blankets and wood carving.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The people are extremely friendly and welcoming.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We deliberately keep the parties small in size to minimize disruption to people and landscape.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I hope that there are still some people interested.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I will be distributing leaflets at the end where you can find out more information, but just for the moment, I'll outline the itinerary,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: the main high points of the holiday.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Obviously you'll start by flying out to Kishba, the capital city on day one.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After a couple of days to acclimatize yourself.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll start the trek on day 3, walking through the enormous Katiba forest, which will take the whole of the day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Day 4 takes us higher up, going through the foothills, past a number of villages.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And visiting a school for the disabled in Sohan.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then you have a rest day, that's day 5, before going to the spectacular Kumi temple, with twelfth-century carvings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Set in a small forest by a lake, and that's day 6, the highlight for many.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We stay near there for day 7, because then comes the hardest day, walking through very mountainous country, but culminating in a swim in the Parteh Falls.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is the highest waterfall in the region.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Day 9 is much easier with part of the day spent in a village where they make some of the gorgeous red blankets, then back down to Kishba, and the journey home.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So you can see it's a pretty packed timetable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2, you will hear a talk given by a group offering a walking holiday to raise money for charity.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Good morning, I'm very pleased to have been invited along to your club to talk about our charity sponsored walking holiday for education aid.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I'll start by giving you a brief overview of what it entails.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. First of all, let me explain what we mean by sponsored here.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is where people promise to donate money to the charity if you achieve your goal, in this case to walk a certain number of miles.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Basically we are organizing a 10 day holiday, from the 6th to the 16th of November, with eight days actual walking, trekking in the Semira mountains.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's have a look at some of the details.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We require you to raise sponsorship money of at least $3,200 paying $250 of it upfront as a deposit.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the rest in stages throughout the year.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Out of this about 35% will go on your expenses, and that leaves 65% guaranteed to go to the charity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which brings me to the most important part.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This trek is being specifically organized to help education in the Semira region.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Last year, we helped train teachers for the disabled.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And this year we're focusing on the pupils.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Each of the walkers' sponsorship money will go to help an individual special needs pupil in one of the mountain schools.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the second part of the talk, I'll be giving you a lot more details.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But back to the basic information.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Age limits.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is the second time we've run this kind of holiday, and on the first we even had an 80 year old, but we found it was wise to establish limits this time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You have to be at least 18, and the top limit is now 70.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Though you need to obtain a health certificate from your doctor if you are over 60 years old.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, the Semira Mountains are among the highest in the world, but you mustn't be too daunted.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We will mainly be trekking in the foothills only, although there will be spectacular views even in the foothills.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, you will need to be extremely fit, if you aren't now, and you're interested in coming with us.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You have plenty of time to get into shape.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll be sleeping in tents, so you must have quite a bit of equipment with you, but you will be helped by local assistants.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Your bedding and so forth will be carried by them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We ask that you only walk with a small rucksack with needs for the day.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I don't think I've really said enough about the marvelous area you'll be walking in.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's have a look at some of the sights you'll be seeing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Apart from these spectacular snow covered peaks and valleys, there are marvelous historic villages.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The area has been famous for centuries for making beautiful carpets, although recently there has been a trend to move into weaving blankets and wood carving.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The people are extremely friendly and welcoming.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We deliberately keep the parties small in size to minimize disruption to people and landscape.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: I hope that there are still some people interested.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I will be distributing leaflets at the end where you can find out more information, but just for the moment, I'll outline the itinerary,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: the main high points of the holiday.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Obviously you'll start by flying out to Kishba, the capital city on day one.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After a couple of days to acclimatize yourself.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll start the trek on day 3, walking through the enormous Katiba forest, which will take the whole of the day.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Day 4 takes us higher up, going through the foothills, past a number of villages.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And visiting a school for the disabled in Sohan.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then you have a rest day, that's day 5, before going to the spectacular Kumi temple, with twelfth-century carvings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Set in a small forest by a lake, and that's day 6, the highlight for many.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We stay near there for day 7, because then comes the hardest day, walking through very mountainous country, but culminating in a swim in the Parteh Falls.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is the highest waterfall in the region.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Day 9 is much easier with part of the day spent in a village where they make some of the gorgeous red blankets, then back down to Kishba, and the journey home.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So you can see it's a pretty packed timetable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

What proportion of the sponsorship money goes to charity?
Each walker's sponsorship money goes to one
When you start the trek you must be
As you walk you will carry
The Semira Region has a long tradition of

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a seminar on ocean research given by a climate scientist.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Thanks to all of you for coming along today to hear about how the Robotic Float project is helping with ocean research.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Well, first of all, we'll look at what a robotic float does and its use.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: So let's start with the device itself.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: It looks a bit like a cigar, and it's about one and a half meters long.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: More importantly, it's full of equipment that's designed to collect data.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: So it can help us in building up a profile of different factors which work together within the world's oceans.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Sounds like a big project, isn't it too big for one country to undertake?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: That's quite true, but this project is a really good example of international cooperation.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Over the last five years, scientists from 13 countries have been taking part in the project, and launching floats in their area of ocean control.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: And next year, this number will rise to 14 when Indonesia joins the project.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: That's impressive.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: But let's move on to how floats work.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: The operational cycle goes like this, each of the floats is dropped in the ocean from a boat at a set point and activated from a satellite.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Then the float immediately sinks about 2,000 meters.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: That's two whole kilometers down in the water.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: It stays at this depth for about 10 days, and is carried around by the currents which operate in the ocean at this level.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: During this time, it's possible for it to cover quite large distances, but the average is 50 km.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: So, what is it actually recording?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Well, at this stage nothing.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: But as it rises to the surface, it collects all sorts of data.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Most importantly variations in salinity, that's salt levels, and the changes in temperature, a bit like underwater weather balloons.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Then, when it gets back to the surface, all the data it's collected is beamed up to the satellite.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: After about 5 hours on the surface, the float automatically sinks, beginning the whole process again.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: What happens to the data?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Well, the information is transferred direct to onshore meteorological stations, like our one in Hobart, and within four hours the findings can be on computers.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: And they can be mapped and analyzed.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the seminar, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You say you're building models of the world's ocean systems, but how're they going to be used, and more importantly when?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Uh. Some of the data has already helped in completing projects.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: For example, our understanding of the underlying causes of El Nino events is being confirmed by float data.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Another way we are using float data is to help us to understand the mechanics of climate change.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Like global warming and ozone depletion.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: That's part of an ongoing variability study, but the results are still a long way off.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: However, this is not the case with our ocean weather forecasting.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Because we know from the floats what the prevailing weather conditions will be in certain parts of the ocean, we can advise the navy on search and rescue missions.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: That's happening right now.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: And many yachtsmen owe their lives to the success of this project.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: In addition, the float data can help us to look at the biological implications of ocean processes.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: Would that help with preserving fish stocks?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Yes, and advising governments on fisheries legislation.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: We're well on the way to completing a project on this.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: We hope it will help to bring about more sustainable fishing practices.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: We'll be seeing the results of that quite soon.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: It sounds like the data from floats has lots of applications.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Yes, it does.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: It's also a powerful agricultural tool.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: If we were aware of what the weather would be like, say next year, we could make sure that the farmers planted appropriate grain varieties to produce the best yield from the available rainfall.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: That sounds a bit like science fiction.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Especially when now we can't even tell them when a drought will break.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: I agree that this concept is still a long way in the future, but it will come eventually.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: And the float data will have made a contribution.
audioscript.speaker: SIMON

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: The Luxury End of the Hotel Business

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear part of a lecture about hotels and the tourist industry.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Today's lecture forms part of the hospitality and tourism module.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Last week I looked at the economy end of the hotel business, this week I'm going to discuss the luxury end of the market.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Let's consider the following scenario.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: You wake up in the middle of the night.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: In a strange hotel, miles away from home, disoriented most probably from jet lag, when even the most expensive surroundings can seem empty and dispiriting.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: You have paid a great deal of money to stay in this first class hotel with its contemporary technology, but according to recent research carried out by an international travel and public relations company.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: All is not well.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The research suggests that even the most opulent, luxurious hotels seem to have underestimated the most basic needs of their customers.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Be they traveling for work or pleasure, the need to feel at home in surroundings which are both familiar and inviting.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Do these findings, however, apply only to hotels situated in particular areas?
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Is it possible that the external environment can affect a guest's well-being?
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The company's research covered a whole range of different hotel types, both independent hotels and those which are part of large chains.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: They investigated chic, so called boutique hotels in the heart of downtown business districts, stately mansions located in the depths of beautiful countryside.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And plush hotels built at the edge of tropical beaches surrounded by palm trees and idyllic blue ocean.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And the research concluded that what was outside the hotel building simply didn't matter.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: This is a fascinating revelation, and those of you hoping to move into careers in the travel and leisure industry would be well advised to look at the findings in more detail.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: But back to the main point of this lecture, the need to feel at home.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: What can the hotel industry do about it?
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And is the very idea so subjective that it's impossible to do anything about it on a global basis?
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: However, nothing stands still in this world.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: One company has come up with a slogan, 'Take Your Home With You', and aims to provide clients with luxury serviced apartments.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Those in the business travel industry maintain that these serviced apartments dispense with all the unwanted and expensive hotel services that business travelers don't want.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: While maximizing the facilities they do want.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: For example, not only sleeping and living accommodation, but also a sleek modern kitchen.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: That allows guests to cook and entertain if they wish at no additional cost.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The attractions of such facilities are obvious, and it'll be interesting to see whether the company manages to establish a trend all over the world and make a lasting impact on the luxury accommodation market.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Now finally, I want to consider the psychology underpinning the traditional holiday hotel industry.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: As a hotelier, how do you go about attracting people to give up the security of their own home, and entrust themselves to staying in a completely strange place?
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And sleeping in an unfamiliar bed.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Firstly, hotels exploit people's need to escape the predictability of their everyday lives.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: For a few days, people can pretend they are free of responsibilities.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And can indulge themselves.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Secondly, there is something very powerful in our need to be pampered and looked after.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: It's almost as if we return to being a baby when everything was done for us, and we felt safe.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And secure, and not far removed from this is the pleasure in being spoiled and given little treats, like the minuscule bottles of shampoo and tiny bars of soap.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The chocolate on your pillow at night, and we actually forget that we are paying for it all.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Next week, I'm gonna look at eco-hotels, a fairly new phenomenon, but increasingly popular...
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: Do these findings, however, apply only to hotels situated in particular areas?
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: Is it possible that the external environment can affect a guest's well-being?
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: The company's research covered a whole range of different hotel types, both independent hotels and those which are part of large chains.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: They investigated chic, so called boutique hotels in the heart of downtown business districts, stately mansions located in the depths of beautiful countryside.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: And plush hotels built at the edge of tropical beaches surrounded by palm trees and idyllic blue ocean.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: And the research concluded that what was outside the hotel building simply didn't matter.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: This is a fascinating revelation, and those of you hoping to move into careers in the travel and leisure industry would be well advised to look at the findings in more detail.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: But back to the main point of this lecture, the need to feel at home.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: What can the hotel industry do about it?
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: And is the very idea so subjective that it's impossible to do anything about it on a global basis?
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: However, nothing stands still in this world.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: One company has come up with a slogan, 'Take Your Home With You', and aims to provide clients with luxury serviced apartments.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: Those in the business travel industry maintain that these serviced apartments dispense with all the unwanted and expensive hotel services that business travelers don't want.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: While maximizing the facilities they do want.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: For example, not only sleeping and living accommodation, but also a sleek modern kitchen.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: That allows guests to cook and entertain if they wish at no additional cost.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: The attractions of such facilities are obvious, and it'll be interesting to see whether the company manages to establish a trend all over the world and make a lasting impact on the luxury accommodation market.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: Now finally, I want to consider the psychology underpinning the traditional holiday hotel industry.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: As a hotelier, how do you go about attracting people to give up the security of their own home, and entrust themselves to staying in a completely strange place?
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: And sleeping in an unfamiliar bed.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: Firstly, hotels exploit people's need to escape the predictability of their everyday lives.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: For a few days, people can pretend they are free of responsibilities.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: And can indulge themselves.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: Secondly, there is something very powerful in our need to be pampered and looked after.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: It's almost as if we return to being a baby when everything was done for us, and we felt safe.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: And secure, and not far removed from this is the pleasure in being spoiled and given little treats, like the minuscule bottles of shampoo and tiny bars of soap.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: The chocolate on your pillow at night, and we actually forget that we are paying for it all.
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: Next week, I'm gonna look at eco-hotels, a fairly new phenomenon, but increasingly popular...
utterances.speaker: LECTURER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

offer an individual and personal welcome.
